topic: Why Venus Has No Moon But Earth Does
title: How Gravity, Formation, and Orbital Stability Shaped Venus

lesson.learn: Venus does not have a moon, while Earth does, and that difference likely comes from how the two planets formed and evolved. Large moons usually come from giant impacts, capture events, or long-term stable satellite systems, and those pathways seem to have been less favorable for Venus. Venus is also closer to the Sun, which means its gravitational environment is different and its long-term satellite stability is less forgiving. The absence of a moon affects Venus's tides, rotation history, and the way scientists compare it with Earth.
lesson.simpler: Earth got a big moon, but Venus did not. That likely happened because the conditions around Venus were not right for a moon to form or survive.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Venus probably lacks a moon because its early history and close-in orbit made long-term moon formation and survival unlikely.
lesson.deeperDive: Moon formation is a delicate problem. A planet needs enough material in orbit after a collision or capture, and that material also has to remain dynamically stable for a very long time. Earth's Moon is thought to have formed from a giant impact, but Venus may not have experienced the same kind of event, or any resulting satellite system may have been destabilized later. Close to the Sun, tidal forces and solar perturbations can make small moons harder to keep. Venus also has an unusual rotation and a dense atmosphere, which complicate models of its long-term evolution. Researchers study these differences because Venus and Earth started from similar building blocks, yet ended up in very different states.
lesson.funFact: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus, which makes its rotation one of the strangest in the Solar System.
references: Planetary science reviews on moon formation
Johns Hopkins and JPL Venus mission materials
Earth and Venus comparative planetology studies

places: Orbit modeling labs - satellite stability research
